Q13 MCQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +1 / -0.33
Not attempted

Suppose you are given a dataset of cellular images from patients with and without cancer. If you are required to train a classifier that predicts the probability that the patient has cancer, you would prefer to use Decision trees over logistic regression.

Correct answer: B
Solution

(b)
False. Decision trees only provide a label estimate, whereas logistic regression provides the
probability of a label (patient has cancer) for a given input (cellular image).
Logistic Regression is a statistical method used for binary classification. It is a type of regression
analysis that models the probability of an outcome occurring based on one or more predictor
variables. The logistic function, also known as the sigmoid function, is employed to map the
linear combination of predictor variables to a probability value between 0 and 1. This probability
can then be converted into a binary outcome, typically 0 or 1.

the event of choosing the die with 1 red face and 5 black faces.
Additionally, let R be the event that the first roll comes up red.
What is the conditional probability that I chose the die with 3 red faces (D1), given that the first roll came up red?

Correct answer: B

Q16 MCQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +1 / -0.33
Not attempted

As the number of training examples tends to infinity, what will happen to the bias of a model trained on that data?

Correct answer: C
Solution

(c)
When the number of training examples increases, the model tends to capture the underlying
patterns in the data more accurately, leading to a reduction in bias. However, as the number of
examples approaches infinity, the model’s bias may stabilize, and further increases might not
significantly impact bias. Therefore, the bias is expected to remain relatively constant as the training
dataset size becomes very large.

Q32 MSQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +2 / -0
Not attempted

Four statements are given, How many matches are correct?

Correct answer: B, C, D
Solution

(b, c, d)
Scenario 1: The experiment involves multiple trials (coin tosses) with a binary outcome (head or tail). The number of successes (heads) in a fixed number of trials follows a Binomial distribution.
Correct Answer: (b) Binomial Distribution
Scenario 2: The scenario describes a continuous random variable representing the time taken,
and longer times being less likely. This is indicative of an Exponential distribution.
Correct Answer: (b) Exponential Distribution
Scenario 3: The scenario involves counting the number of events (cars passing through a toll
booth) in a fixed time period, where the average rate is known. This is a classic case of the Poisson
distribution.
Correct Answer: (a) Poisson Distribution
Scenario 4: The scenario describes a single toss of a biased die with a binary outcome (getting a
6 or not). This is a Bernoulli distribution.
Correct Answer: (b) Bernoulli Distribution

Q33 MCQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +2 / -0.66
Not attempted

The keys 1, 5, 6, 11, 19 have been inserted one by one in some unknown order into an initial

 
 
How many permutations of 1, 5, 6, 11, 19 will generate particular BST?

empty BST. The result is

Correct answer: B
Solution

(b)
1. Choose the Root (6):
• The root of the BST is 6. So, in any permutation, the number 6 must be at the root position.
2. Permute the Left Subtree (Keys < 6):
• The left subtree has keys 1 and 5. These can be arranged in any order.
• Permutations of (1, 5) are: (1, 5) and (5, 1).
3. Permute the Right Subtree (Keys > 6):
• The right subtree has keys 11 and 19. These can be arranged in any order.
• Permutations of (11, 19) are: (11, 19) and (19, 11).
4. Combine the Permutations:
• Combine the permutations from the left subtree, the root (6), and the permutations from
the right subtree.
The resulting permutations are:
• (1, 5, 6, 11, 19)
• (5, 1, 6, 11, 19)
• (11, 19, 6, 5, 1)
• (19, 11, 6, 5, 1)
• (11, 6, 19, 5, 1)
• (19, 6, 11, 5,1)

Q38 NAT Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +1 / -0
Not attempted

What will be number of spanning tree possible for complete graph with 7 vertex

NAT
Correct answer: 16807
Solution

16807
The number of spanning trees for a complete graph with n vertices can be calculated using Cayley’s
formula. Cayley’s formula states that the number of spanning trees in a complete graph with
n vertices is nn– 2.
For a complete graph with 7 vertices:
Number of spanning trees = 77 – 2 = 75 = 16807
Therefore, the number of spanning trees for a complete graph with 7 vertices is 16,807.

Q39 MCQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +1 / -0
Not attempted

What is the primary purpose of a heuristic function in the context of search algorithms?
A. To guarantee an optimal solution.
B. To ensure completeness of the search.
C. To provide a lower bound on the cost to reach the goal.
D. To eliminate the need for a search algorithm.

Correct answer: C
Solution

(c)
A heuristic function is used in search algorithms to estimate the cost from a current state to the
goal state. It provides a heuristic value (or estimate) that guides the search toward potentially
promising paths. The primary purpose of a heuristic function is to provide a lower bound on the
cost to reach the goal, helping the algorithm make informed decisions during the search process.

Q40 MSQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +1 / -0
Not attempted

What about Central Limit Theorem is/are correct?

Correct answer: A, C
Solution

(a, c)
Option (a) States that the distribution of the sum (or average) of a large number of independent,
identically distributed random variables approaches a normal distribution.
Option (c) Useful for making inferences about population parameters.

Q42 MSQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +2 / -0
Not attempted

How many statements are FALSE?

Correct answer: A, B, C
Solution

(a, b, c)
Support Vector Machines, similar to logistic regression models, provide a probability distribution
over the potential labels for a given input example.
Answer with Explanation:
The statement is incorrect. Support Vector Machines (SVMS) are not inherently probabilistic models
like logistic regression. While SVMs can be used for classification tasks. Their primary objective is
to find the hyperplane that best separates different classes. SVMs do not naturally provide a
probability distribution over class labels.
However, there is a variant called “Support Vector Machines for Classification” (SVC) that can be
used in a probabilistic manner. By enabling the ‘probability’ parameter in some implementations
of SVM (e.g., scikit-learn’s SVC), the model can output probability estimates using techniques like
Platt scaling. This allows SVM to provide probability scores, but it’s important to note that SVMs
are not originally designed for probabilistic predictions.
In general, as we transition from a linear Kernel to higher-order polynomial Kernels, we anticipate
the support vectors to largely remain unchanged.
Answer with Explanation:
The statement suggests that when moving from a linear Kernel to higher-order polynomial kernels.
the set of support vectors is expected to remain relatively consistent.

Explanation:
• Support vectors are data points that play a crucial role in determining the decision boundary
in SVM. They are the instances closest to the decision boundary and contribute to the definition
of the margin.
• When using a linear Kernel, the decision boundary is a hyperplane in the original feature
space.
• When transitioning to higher-order polynomial kernels, the decision boundary becomes more
flexible, allowing for a better fit to nom linear relationships in the data.
• However, in many cases, the support vectors that lie near the decision boundary in the linear
case may continue to be influential in the higher-dimensional feature space. Their relative
importance and positions may be preserved, leading to a substantial overlap in the sets of
support vectors.
While the exact behavior can depend on the specific data distribution and the chosen Kernel
parameters, the statement reflects a common expectation in SVM analysis.
Support Vector Machines construct decision boundaries with maximum margins, and these
boundaries generally result in lower generalization errors compared to other linear classifiers.
Explanation:
• Support Vector Machines (SVMs) aim to find decision boundaries with maximum margins,
which are the widest possible separations between different classes in the feature space.
• The concept of maximum margin contributes to better generalization performance. A wider
margin indicates a more robust decision boundary that is less sensitive to small variations in
the training data.
• The intuition is that a decision boundary with a larger margin is more likely to generalize
well to unseen data because it provides a greater buffer against noise or outliers.
• SVMs prioritize finding the hyperplane that not only separates classes but also maximizes the
distance between the nearest data points of different classes (support vectors).
• While the statement emphasizes the advantage of maximum margin decision boundaries, it’s
important to note that the effectiveness of SVMs depends on factors such as the choice of
Kernel and regularization parameters, as well as the characteristics of the data.

Q43 MCQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +1 / -0
Not attempted

Consider a B-tree and a B+ tree with the same order n = 50, each maintaining keys in sorted order. Both trees have been built on a dataset with a large number of records. Evaluate the statements below and choose the correct options:
1. In a B tree, internal nodes can have more than n – 1 keys.
2. The number of children for an internal node in a B tree can range from 2 to n.
3. A B-tree is more efficient in range queries compared to a B+ tree.
4. In a B+ tree, all keys are present in the leaf nodes.
5. A B+ tree has a higher fan-out than a B tree of the same order.
6. In a B+tree, a range query can be performed more efficiently than in a B tree.
Choose the correct?

Correct answer: C
Solution

(c)
1. In a B tree, internal nodes can have more than n – 1 keys. (True, internal nodes can have up to
2n – 1 keys).
2. The number of children for an internal node in a B tree can range from 2 to n. (True, it ranges
from 2 to 2n).
3. A B-tree is more efficient in range queries compared to a B+ tree. (False, B+ trees are generally
more efficient for range queries due to the structure).
4. In a B+ tree, all keys are present in the leaf nodes. (True, all keys are in the leaf nodes, and
internal nodes store only pointers).
5. A B+ tree has a higher fan-out than a B tree of the same order. (True, B+ trees typically have
a higher fan-out because keys are only in the leaf nodes).
6. In a B+tree, a range query can be performed more efficiently than in a B tree. (True, due to
the structure of B+ trees, range queries are more efficient).

Q47 MSQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +2 / -0
Not attempted

Which of the following graphs DOES have an Eulerian circuit? (Recall that an Eulerian circuit in an undirected graph is a walk in the graph that starts of a vertex and returns to the vertex after travelling on each edge exactly once).

Correct answer: B, C, D
Solution

(b, c, d)
A connected undirected multi graph has Euler’s circuit iff all vertices are of Even degree
(a) K9, 9 is Bipartite graph with each side having 9 vertices and each of the vertex on one side are
connected to all vertices of other side, hence degree of all vertices are 9 which is odd degree.
Therefore No Eulers circuit
(b) and (c) K8, 8 and K12, 12 is Bipartite graph with each side having 8 vertices and each of them are
connected to all vertices of other side, hence degree of all vertices are 8 and 12 respectively which
is even. Therefore Eulers Circuit exists
(d) K9 complete graph with 9 vertices with each vertex connected to all other therefore degree is 8.
Therefore Eulers Circuit exists

Q48 MSQ Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +2 / -0
Not attempted

Consider the relations R(A, B, C) and S(B, C, D) and the following SQL queries:
I. Select distinct * from R, S;
II. Select distinct * from R, S where R. C = S. C;
III. Select distinct * from R, S where R. C = S. C and R. BS. B;
IV. Select distinct * from R, S where R. C != S.C;
Match the following table where the first column refers to the four given SQL queries.

 
Correct answer: A, B, C, D
Solution

(a, b, c, d)
I. Select distinct * from R, S;
This will output the cross-product of R and S, as no common attribute is used.
II. Select distinct * from R, S where R. C = S. C;
This is a conditional join where an equality operator (=) is used on a common attribute (C).
Therefore, it is an equi-join.
III. Select distinct * from R, S where R. C = S. C and R. BS. B;
This is an example of a natural join because here common attributes (C, B) are used with a
logical AND.
IV. Select distinct * from R, S where R. C != S.C;
This is a classic example of a conditional join or theta join.

Consider the MADE EASY University, and the relation “took” with attributes (StudentlD, CourseNo, Quarter, Year, Units, Grade), which contains the grades for courses completed by
MADE EASY students over the last two decades. Assume there are 25,000 students enrolled each quarter, and each student takes four courses per quarter, with four quarters in a year. This results in a total of 8,000,000 records.
Additionally, 10,000 new students join MADE EASY University every year. Consequently, in the “took” relation, there are 200,000 distinct students, each identified by a unique StudentlD. On average, a student takes 40 different courses. The file blocks have a size of 2048 bytes, and each tuple in the “took” relation requires 50 bytes.
The table has a primary index on StudentlD, implemented as a B+ tree with an order n = 101. In the worst case, how many levels does the B+ tree use?

NAT
Correct answer: 4
Solution

4

 
 
Q60 NAT Not checked
Technical • Data Science & Artificial Intelligence • +2 / -0
Not attempted

Consider a robot operating in the two-cell grid world shown below. Suppose the robot is initially in the cell C1. At any point of time the robot can execute any of the two actions: A1 and A2. A1 is “to move to a neighboring cell”. If the robot is in C1 the action A1 succeeds (moves the robot into C1) with the probability 0.9 and fails (leaves the robot in C1) with the probability 0.1. If the robot is in C2 the action A1 succeeds (moves the robot into C1) with the probability 0.8 and fails (leaves the robot in C2) with the probability 0.2. The action A2 is “to stay in the same cell”, and when executed it keeps the robot in the same cell with probability 1. The first action the robot executes is chosen at random (with an equal probability between A1 and A2). Afterwards, the robot alternates the actions it executes. (For example, if the robot executed at ion A1 first, then the sequence of actions is A1, A2, A1, A2, ...). Answer the following questions.

 
 
Suppose you were told that the first action the robot executes is A1. What is the probability that the robot will appear in cell C1 after it executes close to infinitely many actions?
NAT
Correct answer: 0.4706
Solution

0.4706

11

You are lost in the National park of Jim Corbett. The park population consists of tourists and Jim Corbett. Tourists comprise two-thirds of the population the park and give a correct answer to requests for directions with probability. The air of Jim Corbett has an amnesiac quality, however, and so the answers to repeated questions to tourists are independent, even if the question and the person are the same. If you ask a
Jim Corbett for directions, the answer is always wrong. Suppose you ask a randomly chosen passer-by whether the exit from the park is East or West. The answer is East. You then ask the same person again, and the reply is again East. What is the probability of East being correct?

NAT
Correct answer: 0.5
Solution

0.5
